sectionName: Psychological, Social & Biological Foundations of Behavior

questions.passage: Attrition bias in a school-based mental-health program. Researchers examined whether a school mental-health program appears effective after accounting for differential attrition using a design intended to connect a psychological or sociological construct with a measurable behavioral outcome. The study included 24 schools assigned to a program or waitlist comparison with follow-up surveys and compared Waitlist retained, Program retained, Program all assigned. The investigators selected these conditions because they differed in program assignment and analytic handling of participants lost to follow-up, while keeping the setting, instructions, and timing of the outcome assessment similar across groups. The primary outcome was change in symptom score from baseline to follow-up; a secondary measure was attendance and reason for missing follow-up. Participants also completed baseline questionnaires measuring baseline symptoms, school size, and counselor availability, which allowed the investigators to check whether preexisting differences might explain the pattern of results. Figure 1 summarizes the main outcome. The overall pattern was that the retained-only estimate overstated improvement compared with an all-assigned estimate. The authors interpreted this pattern as evidence related to program evaluation, attrition bias, and intention-to-treat reasoning, but they cautioned that the study did not measure every possible mechanism. For example, nonrandom dropout related to symptom severity could also affect the outcome unless future work specifically controls or manipulates it. The investigators therefore described the results as supporting a particular interpretation rather than proving that a single mental state or social process fully explained every observed difference. The study also included a manipulation check. Participants in the condition most directly targeting program evaluation, attrition bias, and intention-to-treat reasoning reported greater change in retained-only versus all-assigned improvement estimates than participants in the comparison condition, whereas baseline scores did not differ meaningfully across groups. The authors argued that this pattern increased confidence that the intervention changed the intended construct. However, they noted that self-report measures can be influenced by demand characteristics and that behavior in a study setting may not perfectly generalize to natural settings. In a follow-up analysis, the authors tested whether baseline symptom severity altered the size of the effect. This analysis was exploratory and was not powered to make strong subgroup claims. The authors suggested that a more rigorous next step would be using preregistered intention-to-treat analyses and multiple-imputation sensitivity checks. They also warned that treating the figure as a simple proof of a broad theory would overstate the evidence, because the passage provides only a limited comparison among specific operational definitions and outcomes. The investigators emphasized that the variables were operational definitions of broader constructs rather than direct measurements of every psychological or social process that might be relevant. Participants completed baseline measures before randomization or grouping, and the investigators checked whether the comparison groups differed on prior exposure, demographic characteristics, and initial scores on the main outcome. The passage is therefore intended to support reasoning about construct validity, internal validity, and the difference between an association shown by a figure and a broader causal claim that would require additional evidence.
questions.stem: Which interpretation of Figure 1 is most consistent with the investigators' central claim?

choices.A: The figure proves that nonrandom dropout related to symptom severity played no role, because any randomized or comparison-group design eliminates all unmeasured mechanisms.
choices.B: The outcome pattern supports the claim that changing program evaluation, attrition bias, and intention-to-treat reasoning can alter change in symptom score from baseline to follow-up in this setting, while still leaving some alternative mechanisms unresolved.
choices.C: The study cannot support any inference about program evaluation, attrition bias, and intention-to-treat reasoning because psychological and social constructs cannot be operationalized with measurable outcomes.
choices.D: The safest conclusion is that the comparison group with the lowest mean performed best, because smaller bars or lower scores are always favorable in behavioral figures. This would be a recognizable misconception because it treats program evaluation, attrition bias, and intention-to-treat reasoning as a label rather than linking it to the passage's actual measurement strategy.

explanations.A: This choice is tempting because it highlights a real issue in research on program evaluation, attrition bias, and intention-to-treat reasoning, but it does not fit the passage as well as the credited answer. The option says, 'The figure proves that nonrandom dropout related to symptom severity played no role, because any randomized or comparison-group design eliminates all unmeasured mechanisms.' Yet the study manipulated program assignment and analytic handling of participants lost to follow-up and measured change in symptom score from baseline to follow-up, so the stronger inference must connect those operational definitions to the figure. A student might pick this if they recognized a related term but ignored whether the design actually supports that specific claim. The passage leaves room for nonrandom dropout related to symptom severity, but that limitation does not reverse the observed pattern or erase the construct being tested.
explanations.C: This choice is tempting because it highlights a real issue in research on program evaluation, attrition bias, and intention-to-treat reasoning, but it does not fit the passage as well as the credited answer. The option says, 'The study cannot support any inference about program evaluation, attrition bias, and intention-to-treat reasoning because psychological and social constructs cannot be operationalized with measurable outcomes.' Yet the study manipulated program assignment and analytic handling of participants lost to follow-up and measured change in symptom score from baseline to follow-up, so the stronger inference must connect those operational definitions to the figure. A student might pick this if they recognized a related term but ignored whether the design actually supports that specific claim. The passage leaves room for nonrandom dropout related to symptom severity, but that limitation does not reverse the observed pattern or erase the construct being tested.

The option says, 'The safest conclusion is that the comparison group with the lowest mean performed best, because smaller bars or lower scores are always favorable in behavioral figures. This would be a recognizable misconception because it treats program evaluation, attrition bias, and intention-to-treat reasoning as a label rather than linking it to the passage's actual measurement strategy.' Yet the study manipulated program assignment and analytic handling of participants lost to follow-up and measured change in symptom score from baseline to follow-up, so the stronger inference must connect those operational definitions to the figure. A student might pick this if they recognized a related term but ignored whether the design actually supports that specific claim. explanations.B: Correct. This answer best integrates the design, Figure 1, and the construct being tested. The passage says the groups differed in program assignment and analytic handling of participants lost to follow-up and that the primary outcome was change in symptom score from baseline to follow-up; the figure then shows that the retained-only estimate overstated improvement compared with an all-assigned estimate. That combination supports the inference in the answer without overstating it as proof of every possible mechanism. It also respects the distinction between a construct, such as program evaluation, attrition bias, and intention-to-treat reasoning, and the way that construct was operationalized through retained-only versus all-assigned improvement estimates and the measured outcome. questions.topic: Attrition bias in a school-based mental-health program
questions.passageImageCaption: Figure 1. Improvement estimates among retained participants versus all assigned participants.

questions.stem: In this study, retained-only versus all-assigned improvement estimates is best described as which type of variable?

choices.A: It is an operationalized measure of program evaluation, attrition bias, and intention-to-treat reasoning, meaning it is a concrete indicator used to study a broader theoretical construct.
choices.B: It is the independent variable only because all self-report measures are automatically assigned by the researcher rather than measured from participants.
choices.C: It is a biological confound that makes the social construct irrelevant, since subjective ratings can never be used in behavioral research.
choices.D: It is the sampling frame, because any questionnaire completed before the outcome determines which participants entered the study. This would be a recognizable misconception because it treats program evaluation, attrition bias, and intention-to-treat reasoning as a label rather than linking it to the passage's actual measurement strategy.

questions.topic: Attrition bias in a school-based mental-health program

questions.stem: Which additional finding would most strengthen the proposed mechanism rather than merely repeating the same outcome?

choices.A: Showing that changes in retained-only versus all-assigned improvement estimates statistically mediate the effect of condition on change in symptom score from baseline to follow-up would better connect the manipulation to the proposed mechanism.
choices.B: Showing that every participant preferred the intervention would prove the mechanism, even if preference was unrelated to change in symptom score from baseline to follow-up.
choices.C: Showing that baseline scores differed strongly between groups would strengthen the causal interpretation by making the groups easier to separate.
choices.D: Removing the manipulation check would strengthen the study because fewer measures always reduce the risk of measurement bias. This would be a recognizable misconception because it treats program evaluation, attrition bias, and intention-to-treat reasoning as a label rather than linking it to the passage's actual measurement strategy.

questions.topic: Attrition bias in a school-based mental-health program

questions.stem: Which limitation should most temper the authors' conclusion about program evaluation, attrition bias, and intention-to-treat reasoning?

choices.A: The study is invalid because any use of a comparison condition prevents researchers from drawing conclusions about group differences. This would be a recognizable misconception because it treats program evaluation, attrition bias, and intention-to-treat reasoning as a label rather than linking it to the passage's actual measurement strategy.
choices.B: The design does not fully rule out nonrandom dropout related to symptom severity, so the result supports the authors' interpretation but does not uniquely identify one mechanism.
choices.C: The finding is impossible to interpret because a significant behavioral outcome can never coexist with an imperfect manipulation check.
choices.D: The result must be due to random error because all social and psychological interventions produce identical effects across participants.

questions.topic: Attrition bias in a school-based mental-health program

questions.stem: Which follow-up design would best test the generalizability of the result?

choices.A: Repeating the same analysis on the same participants without collecting new data would establish external validity because the sample size would appear larger.
choices.B: Replicating the intervention in a different setting and measuring change in symptom score from baseline to follow-up over a longer follow-up would test whether the pattern extends beyond the original sample.
choices.C: Dropping the comparison group would improve generalizability by making the design more similar to everyday life, even though it weakens interpretation. This would be a recognizable misconception because it treats program evaluation, attrition bias, and intention-to-treat reasoning as a label rather than linking it to the passage's actual measurement strategy.

questions.topic: Attrition bias in a school-based mental-health program

questions.passage: Debriefing and consent in a deception study. Researchers examined whether a deception-based social-stress study meets ethical and methodological requirements using a design intended to connect a psychological or sociological construct with a measurable behavioral outcome. The study included 180 adults assigned to receive evaluative or neutral feedback before a performance task and compared Neutral, Stress no debrief, Stress + debrief. The investigators selected these conditions because they differed in evaluative feedback and whether participants received an immediate debriefing, while keeping the setting, instructions, and timing of the outcome assessment similar across groups. The primary outcome was withdrawal request after learning the study purpose; a secondary measure was reported distress and understanding of debriefing. Participants also completed baseline questionnaires measuring trait anxiety and prior research participation, which allowed the investigators to check whether preexisting differences might explain the pattern of results. Figure 1 summarizes the main outcome. The overall pattern was that deception increased withdrawal requests, while immediate debriefing modestly reduced requests and improved understanding. The authors interpreted this pattern as evidence related to research ethics, deception, and informed consent, but they cautioned that the study did not measure every possible mechanism. For example, distress caused by feedback rather than deception itself could also affect the outcome unless future work specifically controls or manipulates it. The investigators therefore described the results as supporting a particular interpretation rather than proving that a single mental state or social process fully explained every observed difference. The study also included a manipulation check. Participants in the condition most directly targeting research ethics, deception, and informed consent reported greater change in withdrawal request and debriefing understanding than participants in the comparison condition, whereas baseline scores did not differ meaningfully across groups. The authors argued that this pattern increased confidence that the intervention changed the intended construct. However, they noted that self-report measures can be influenced by demand characteristics and that behavior in a study setting may not perfectly generalize to natural settings. In a follow-up analysis, the authors tested whether trait anxiety altered the size of the effect. This analysis was exploratory and was not powered to make strong subgroup claims. The authors suggested that a more rigorous next step would be testing less deceptive alternatives that preserve internal validity while reducing participant distress. They also warned that treating the figure as a simple proof of a broad theory would overstate the evidence, because the passage provides only a limited comparison among specific operational definitions and outcomes. The investigators emphasized that the variables were operational definitions of broader constructs rather than direct measurements of every psychological or social process that might be relevant. Participants completed baseline measures before randomization or grouping, and the investigators checked whether the comparison groups differed on prior exposure, demographic characteristics, and initial scores on the main outcome. The passage is therefore intended to support reasoning about construct validity, internal validity, and the difference between an association shown by a figure and a broader causal claim that would require additional evidence.

choices.A: The outcome pattern supports the claim that changing research ethics, deception, and informed consent can alter withdrawal request after learning the study purpose in this setting, while still leaving some alternative mechanisms unresolved.
choices.B: The figure proves that distress caused by feedback rather than deception itself played no role, because any randomized or comparison-group design eliminates all unmeasured mechanisms.
choices.C: The study cannot support any inference about research ethics, deception, and informed consent because psychological and social constructs cannot be operationalized with measurable outcomes.
choices.D: The safest conclusion is that the comparison group with the lowest mean performed best, because smaller bars or lower scores are always favorable in behavioral figures. questions.topic: Debriefing and consent in a deception study

questions.stem: Which limitation should most temper the authors' conclusion about research ethics, deception, and informed consent?

choices.A: The study is invalid because any use of a comparison condition prevents researchers from drawing conclusions about group differences. This would be a recognizable misconception because it treats research ethics, deception, and informed consent as a label rather than linking it to the passage's actual measurement strategy.
choices.B: The finding is impossible to interpret because a significant behavioral outcome can never coexist with an imperfect manipulation check.
choices.C: The design does not fully rule out distress caused by feedback rather than deception itself, so the result supports the authors' interpretation but does not uniquely identify one mechanism.

questions.topic: Debriefing and consent in a deception study

questions.stem: Which follow-up design would best test the generalizability of the result?

choices.A: Repeating the same analysis on the same participants without collecting new data would establish external validity because the sample size would appear larger.
choices.B: Dropping the comparison group would improve generalizability by making the design more similar to everyday life, even though it weakens interpretation. This would be a recognizable misconception because it treats research ethics, deception, and informed consent as a label rather than linking it to the passage's actual measurement strategy.
choices.C: Replicating the intervention in a different setting and measuring withdrawal request after learning the study purpose over a longer follow-up would test whether the pattern extends beyond the original sample.
